subtitle: Tensor fasciae latae — often confused with IT band

strava_risk_triggers.risk_note: TFL tightness is the most common upstream driver of IT band issues

mild.description: Outer thigh tightness, feels like a tight band from hip to knee
mild.can_run_note: Yes — foam roll TFL before and after every run
why: TFL overuse compensating for weak glute medius
Hip internal rotation increasing TFL tension during stance phase
Sustained sitting in hip flexion shortening the TFL
relief: Foam roll outer quad and TFL — from hip crease downward
Cross-legged forward lean stretch targeting TFL
Side-lying hip abduction to activate the glute med instead
Heat on outer hip before runs
prevention: Glute medius strengthening to reduce TFL compensation
Hip alignment cues during running — avoid hip drop
Regular TFL mobility work as part of cool-down

moderate.description: Persistent outer thigh pulling or aching during and after runs
why: Chronic TFL overload with trigger points causing referred outer thigh pain
IT band tension building from persistent TFL tightness
Underlying hip weakness not being addressed
relief: Daily foam rolling and hip strengthening programme
Dry needling to TFL trigger points
Reduce mileage and prioritise 2 weeks of strength work
prevention: Address glute weakness as the root cause
Lateral band walks and clamshells 3× per week
Gait analysis to identify crossover running pattern

severe.description: Outer thigh pain extending to knee, cannot run without pain
severe.can_run_note: Rest — TFL pain at this level typically means IT band is also affected
why: Full IT band syndrome with TFL as the primary driver
Chronic trigger points requiring hands-on treatment
relief: Physiotherapy for manual therapy and biomechanical assessment
prevention: Establish hip strength base before returning to mileage
Running form correction for hip drop pattern

see_physio_if: Pain extends to the outer knee
No improvement after 2 weeks of foam rolling and strengthening
Pain on both outer thighs simultaneously
